title: Applying flexible decorator's caulk
subtitle: Fill movement gaps at skirting boards, coving, window frames, and architrave with paintable caulk
excerpt: How to use flexible acrylic decorator's caulk to seal the gap between painted woodwork and a wall or ceiling. Covers choosing the right product, cutting the nozzle, and smoothing the bead for a clean painted finish.

glossaryTerms.definition: A water-based acrylic flexible sealant, sold in cartridges, for filling gaps between painted woodwork and walls or ceilings. Remains flexible when set and can be painted over.

content.text: The gap that appears between skirting boards and a wall, or between coving and a ceiling, is a movement gap, the plaster and the timber are expanding and contracting at different rates. Rigid filler in these gaps cracks within a season.
flexes with the movement and stays intact.

Use caulk at every join between a painted timber surface and a plastered surface: skirting-to-wall, coving-to-ceiling, architrave-to-wall, and window-bead-to-reveal. Use it also at plaster cracks that run along a structural line such as a lintel or a change of material. Do not use it on cracks across the middle of a plasterboard bay, which is a taping failure.

Remove all loose filler from the gap with the scraper. Brush out dust. Both surfaces must be dry and free of grease, caulk applied over a wet or greasy surface will not adhere and will peel within weeks.
Step 2, Cut and fit the nozzle

Cut the nozzle at 45° with the utility knife, 3 to 5 mm from the tip for a skirting gap, or 8 to 10 mm from the tip for a wider coving gap. Pierce the foil seal with a nail or the tip of the knife. Load the cartridge into the gun. For small jobs, a trigger-style or sausage-gun squeeze-tube cartridge is easier to control than a standard cartridge gun.
Step 3, Apply and smooth the bead

Run a continuous bead along the joint, angling the nozzle into the gap at 45°. Work steadily, a uniform bead is easier to smooth than a lumpy one. Within a minute of applying, run a wet finger along the bead in a single pass to press the caulk into the gap and feather the edges onto both surfaces. Dip the finger in water before each pass. Keep the movement steady; hesitating partway along the bead leaves a ridge.

Wipe excess caulk off the surrounding surfaces with a damp cloth immediately, uncured caulk wipes off cleanly; cured caulk does not. Leave to dry for at least 2 hours (longer in a cold room) before painting. Most decorator's caulk is fully paintable once dry; check the label for the recommended overcoat time.
What it looks like when it's right
A correctly applied bead is concave, pressed into the gap, and feathered flush with both surfaces. No ridges or blobs. When painted, the joint looks like a clean painted line.
Using the wrong product: silicone sealant does not take paint the same way caulk does. It looks different once painted and is much harder to remove if the junction needs redoing.
Applying over existing caulk that has cracked: remove the old caulk with the scraper and a damp cloth, let the surfaces dry, and re-apply fresh caulk.
Leaving a convex bead rather than a concave one: this means too much caulk was applied. Scrape off the excess while wet and smooth again with a wet finger.
